title: 兩個identical既波受萬有引力影響會係幾耐之後撞埋一齊？

replies.msg: 設波既半徑=R
你up 乜
同埋一開始兩個波既圓心距離=D
假設距離係無限遠 作用力會係0

假設距離係無限遠 作用力會係0

如果有限距離呢

此回覆已被刪除

g = GM/R^2 = acceleration at the point of contact
= acceleration after the ball has displaced for a distance D/2

s = v_0t + at^2
D/2 = (GM/R^2) (t^2)
t = sqrt(DR^2/2GM)

jm9 個acceleration唔係const
此回覆已被刪除

F = Gm^2/R^2

F = ma

a = Gm/R^2

s = 0.5at^2

s_1 = 0.5(Gm/R^2)t_1^2

s_2 = 0.5(Gm/R^2)t_2^2

s_1 + s_2 = D-2R

(t_1^2 + t_2^2)(0.5Gm/R^2) = D-2R

Assume two motions are identical

t_1 = t_2

t = (D-2R)/2(0.5Gm/R^2)

利伸9up

g = GM/R^2 = acceleration at the point of contact
= acceleration after the ball has displaced for a distance D/2

s = v_0t + at^2
D/2 = (GM/R^2) (t^2)
t = sqrt(DR^2/2GM)

The acceleration is not constant, how can you use the equation for constant acceleration?

F = Gm^2/R^2

F = ma

a = Gm/R^2

s = 0.5at^2

s_1 = 0.5(Gm/R^2)t_1^2

s_2 = 0.5(Gm/R^2)t_2^2

s_1 + s_2 = D-2R

(t_1^2 + t_2^2)(0.5Gm/R^2) = D-2R

Assume two motions are identical

t_1 = t_2

t = √[(D-2R)/2(0.5Gm/R^2)]

利伸9up

改返

g = GM/R^2 = acceleration at the point of contact
= acceleration after the ball has displaced for a distance D/2

s = v_0t + at^2
D/2 = (GM/R^2) (t^2)
t = sqrt(DR^2/2GM)

The acceleration is not constant, how can you use the equation for constant acceleration?

係喎

g = GM/R^2 = acceleration at the point of contact
= acceleration after the ball has displaced for a distance D/2

s = v_0t + at^2
D/2 = (GM/R^2) (t^2)
t = sqrt(DR^2/2GM)

if g is NOT a constant
We have v_t = at + C_1 and s_t = at^2/2 + C_2

v_0 =/= 0 (because D =/= infinity)
So C_1 =/=0
That would be a differential equation and thus out of DSE/AL Physics Scope.

I didnt say it is a DSE/AL level question.

p = momentum = integration of F w.r.t time from 0 to t

Why is distance equal to 0 at t=T? Isnt it 2R? And integration of F wrt t should be change of momentum? If momentum is conserved in the process, momentum at any time = initial momentum = 0?

No. External force acting on system = acceleration = not conservation of momentum
